coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots sensitized ZnSn(OH)6 for visible light-driven photocatalytic water purification
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuanyuan
Wang, Lili
Yang, Manli
Wang, Jie
Shi, Jinsheng
coredata.dc:description: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated ZnSn(OH)6 (ZSH) micro-spheres with uniform solid, hollow and yolk-shell structures were successfully synthesized by an etching-second growth strategy and facile hydrothermal method. Compared with pure ZSH, ZSH@CQDs composites exhibited obviously enhanced photocatalytic activities for both degradation of organic pollutant and disinfection of staphylococcus aureus. In CQDs decorated composites, ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure presented 70.4% of RhB degradation efficiency under visible light irradiation within 8 h, which was about 10 times higher than that of ZSH samples. ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure also manifested the best photocatalytic inactivation performance under visible light illumination, which could achieve water purification of 100% bacteria disinfection within 16 h. The enhanced photocatalytic activities might be attributed to the unique up-conversion property of CQDs, as well as its efficient charge separation, and a possible mechanism was reasonably proposed. This work could provide a new perspective to design efficient CQDs decorated photocatalysts for water purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
ZnSn(OH)6
Up-conversion
Efficient charge separation
Photocatalytic water purification

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Controlled fabrication process for three morphologies of ZSH@CQDs composites.
(a)–(f) TEM images of the obtained samples and HR-TEM images of (g) ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure and (h) CQDs.
XPS spectra of ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure: (a) survey spectrum, (b) Zn 2p, (c) Sn 3d and (d) C 1s.
UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of samples and schematic illustration (inset) of multiple light reflections within different structures.

(a) RhB photodegradation curves and (b) corresponding apparent rate constants of six different samples under visible light irradiation; photocatalytic disinfection of different samples under (c) dark and (d) visible light irradiation; (e) bacterial colonies of re-cultured staphylococcus aureus after dealing with ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure at different time.
Up-conversion emission spectra of ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure under different excitation wavelengths.

(a) Comparison of transient photocurrent responses of samples with light on/off cycles under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm, [Na2SO4] = 0.1 M). (b) EIS Nyquist plots of photocatalysts under visible light irradiation (λ > 420 nm) and corresponding schematic (inset) of the equivalent circuit obtained by EIS result fitting.
(a) Photocatalytic disinfection of bacteria and (b) RhB photodegradation over ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure under visible light in presence of different scavengers.
Possible photocatalytic mechanism of ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots sensitized ZnSn(OH)6 for visible light-driven photocatalytic water purification

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science, Qingdao Agricultural University, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qingdao Bona Biomimetic Composite Research Institute Co. Ltd, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qingdao Bona Biomimetic Composite Research Institute Co. Ltd

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Qingdao Agricultural University, Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science, Chengyang District, Qingdao, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decorated ZnSn(OH)6 (ZSH) micro-spheres with uniform solid, hollow and yolk-shell structures were successfully synthesized by an etching-second growth strategy and facile hydrothermal method. Compared with pure ZSH, ZSH@CQDs composites exhibited obviously enhanced photocatalytic activities for both degradation of organic pollutant and disinfection of staphylococcus aureus. In CQDs decorated composites, ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure presented 70.4% of RhB degradation efficiency under visible light irradiation within 8 h, which was about 10 times higher than that of ZSH samples. ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure also manifested the best photocatalytic inactivation performance under visible light illumination, which could achieve water purification of 100% bacteria disinfection within 16 h. The enhanced photocatalytic activities might be attributed to the unique up-conversion property of CQDs, as well as its efficient charge separation, and a possible mechanism was reasonably proposed. This work could provide a new perspective to design efficient CQDs decorated photocatalysts for water purification.

ce:section.ce:para:
With the fast development of industrialization, environmental problems especially wastewater contamination has caused widespread concern because its great threat to aquatic life, human survival and social development [1–3]. Millions of serious diseases even death is caused by them every year [4]. Therefore, rapid and efficient water purification methods are explored to address this global trouble. Photocatalysis, as an efficient, low cost and environmentally friendly strategy, has been one of the promising candidates in comparison with traditional purification methods [5,6]. Based on the reported papers, ZnSn(OH)6 (ZSH), as one of the perovskite materials, has drawn great public attention in photocatalysis [1–4]. Compared with traditional catalysts such as TiO2 and ZnO, ZSH presents much higher photocatalytic activities in organic pollutants degradation, hydrogen evolution and CO2 reduction due to its abundant surface OH− groups, which could utilize photoinduced holes to form highly active
OH radicals. Unfortunately, the band gap of ZSH is about 4.0 eV, largely inhibited its photocatalytic activity because only ultraviolet (UV) can be utilized by ZSH [1,5]. As is well known, UV light only accounts for 5% of solar energy, while the residual 48% and 47% are composed of visible light and near-infrared light, respectively [6,7], which are not well utilized for ZSH-based photocatalysis.

Morphology control is an important way to realize high photon utilization efficiency and efficient photocatalytic activity. According to the reported studies, materials with yolk-shell structure displayed strengthened photocatalytic activity owing to their multi-reflections of light, organic adsorption and porous characteristics [8,9]. These catalysts with higher stability and light adsorption efficiency present remarkable ability in comparison with their conventional hollow and solid counterparts in photocatalytic activity [10]. For example, Sun et al. synthesized Zn2SnO4/SnO2 composites with different morphologies and the result suggested that sample with yolk-shell structure presented higher photocatalytic activity in comparison with other catalysts [8].

In recent years, carbon-based materials, such as carbon nanotubes, activated carbon and graphene, are widely used to support samples for various applications in nanomedicine, bioimaging, as well as photocatalysis [11,12]. As we know, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a newly developed carbon-based zero-dimensional nanomaterial, have aroused widespread attention owing to their unique properties including tunable photoluminescence (PL) emissions, low toxicity, high aqueous solubility, high chemical stability, environmental friendliness and excellent electrical conductivity [13,14]. Particularly, CQDs could act as outstanding spectral harvesters and converters, in which contradiction between light absorption and redox reaction dynamics of numerous UV and visible responsive photocatalysts could be realized [15,16]. This result might be attributed to ability of CQDs, which present a wide range of up-conversion absorption and a large cross-section of PL emission spectra, speeding up energy bandgap matching of a large number of catalysts [17,18]. In addition, CQDs could also suppress recombination of photogenerated charge carriers by capturing photogenerated electrons, thereby prolonging their lifetimes [19,20].

Herein, uniform solid, hollow and yolk-shell ZSH were successfully fabricated by a facile solvothermal method and etching-second growth strategy. CQDs was selected to sensitize ZSH. Compared with pure ZSH, ZSH@CQDs composites exhibited obviously enhanced photocatalytic activities for both degradation of organic pollutant and disinfection of staphylococcus aureus. The enhanced photocatalytic activity could be attributed to the unique up-conversion PL properties of CQDs, as well as efficient charge separation.

ZSH with three different morphologies were prepared according to the reported paper [8] and the specific procedures were shown in Supporting Information. ZSH@CQDs composites were fabricated through a facile hydrothermal method (see Fig. 1 ). Typically, 200 mg of as-prepared ZSH dispersed into 80 mL deionized water by ultrasound. After 30 min, 720 mg glucose as carbon source and 70 mg ascorbic acid as acid catalyst were introduced and stirred for 1 h. Then the mixtures were poured into 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves and kept at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained gray precipitates were collected and washed several times with deionized water and ethanol. Finally, samples were dried at vacuum oven for 12 h.
Pure CQDs was also synthesized by the same method only using glucose and ascorbic acid.

Photocatalytic inactivation of bacteria was investigated under a 300 W Xe lamp equipped with a UV cutoff filter (>420 nm). Staphylococcus aureus was selected as typical bacteria. Firstly, staphylococcus aureus was cultured in LB broth at 37 °C to realize a cell count of approximate 107 colony forming units (CFU)/mL. 80 mg obtained sample was introduced into 30 mL bacteria suspension solution. 1 mL solution was taken out at intervals of 0.5 h then immediately spread on nutrient agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h to examine the number of viable cells.

Photodegradation experiment was also carried out by removing of RhB dye under visible light. Prior to irradiation, 50 mg of catalyst was dispersed in an aqueous solution of RhB (100 mL, 0.01 mM) with magnetic stirring for 30 min in dark condition. During irradiation, 5 mL of suspension was taken and centrifugated at intervals of 30 min. Concentrations of dye were determined by UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1901).

Morphology and microstructure of synthesized samples are investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). In Fig. 2 a, as-prepared ZSH spheres are uniform and their mean size is about 1.3 μm. Besides, their surfaces are relatively smooth and TEM image in Fig. 3 a suggests that the obtained spheres are solid structures. Hollow ZSH spheres could be easily obtained by a simple NaOH etching progress. As can be seen from Fig. 2b, morphology of sample is uniform microsphere after etching treatment. This hollow structure is confirmed by TEM image. As shown in Fig. 3b, obvious contrast of dark edges and pale center could be clearly observed and shell thickness of micro-spheres is in the range of 70–150 nm. As for yolk-shell structure, ZSH dissolution mainly occurs between passivated outer surface of pre-grown seed particles and repeatedly deposited crystal layers, which lead to the formation of yolk-shell. SEM in Fig. 2c suggests that spherical morphology is well preserved and TEM image in Fig. 3c confirms that as-prepared ZSH spheres have an obvious yolk-shell interior structure with a perfectly retained solid yolk. Glucose as precursor and ascorbic acid as acid catalyst for construction of CQDs. After decoration by CQDs, ZSH@CQDs composites (Fig. 2d–f) also present uniform spherical structure and their surfaces become relatively rough. TEM images in Fig. 3d–f further confirm the above results. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3g, a CQDs layer in ZSH@CQDs composite could be clearly seen, which present about 4–5 nm self-assembled CQDs around ZSH and intrinsic morphology of CQDs is depicted in Fig. 3h. Lattice spacing of CQDs is 0.23 nm, agreeing well with (1 0 0) lattice planes of graphite [21]. This result suggests that CQDs have a crystalline graphitic carbon structure.

XPS analysis was studied to determine surface chemical compositions of materials [22]. In Fig. 4 a, main elements of Zn, Sn, O and C could be simultaneously detected. Fig. 4b–d provide high resolution XPS spectra of Sn 3d, Zn 2p and C 1s, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, Zn element presents two characteristic peaks at binding energy values of 1045.6 and 1022.1 eV, which are attributed to Zn 2p1/2 and Zn 2p3/2 for Zn2+ state, respectively [23,24]. This result agrees well with the binding energy of Sn4+ ion [25]. In Fig. 4c, two strong peaks locate at 495.7 and 485.2 eV, which are ascribed to Sn 2p3/2 and Sn 2p5/2 for Sn4+ state, respectively. The split C 1s peak (Fig. 4d) of ZSH@CQDs presents fitting peaks at 288.5, 287.7, 286.3 and 284.7 eV, which are assigned to C(O)O, CO, CO and CC, respectively [26]. These results demonstrate that ZSH@CQDs composites are successfully fabricated.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy is also a helpful strategy to investigate types of functional groups and chemical linkages of samples. In Fig. 5 , CQDs exhibit main stretching bands at 3200, 3000, 2927, 1718, 1640, 1402 and 1164, which are attributed to OH, CH (sp2), CH (sp3), CO, CC, COC and CO, respectively [27]. These stretching bands are also clearly seen in FTIR spectra of ZSH@CQDs. As shown in Fig. 5, three ZSH@CQDs samples present similar IR characteristic and quite resemble to the reported MSn(OH)6 (M = Cu, Mg, Zn) [24,28]. Two vibrations at 540 and 648 cm−1 could be attributed to stretching modes of SnOH, and SnOSn, respectively [29]. In addition, a broad band at about 3200 cm−1 together with a sharp absorption at 1175 cm−1 could be clearly seen, which are ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH groups in ZHS or in CQDs [30] and the OH bending vibration of MOH [28], respectively. Besides, spectral bands at 1632 and 775 cm−1 could be corresponded to OH in-plane deformation vibration and H2O-H2O hydrogen binding absorption, respectively [24]. Moreover, surface carbonate species resulted from the adsorbed CO2 with H2O could be clearly seen at 850 cm−1.

Fig. 6 presents UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of solid, hollow and yolk-shell ZSH samples, together with that of CQDs decorated ZSH composites. Both three pure ZSH have no light absorption within visible light region and their adsorption edges locate at about 330 nm. However, after decoration of CQDs, ZSH@CQDs composites present strong absorption in whole visible light compared with their counterparts, suggesting ZSH@CQDs composites are successfully fabricated. In addition, absorption intensity of ZSH@CQDs gradually enhances with morphology evolution from solid to hollow and yolk-shell. This result indicates that incident light may occur multiple reflection within the particles due to their unique yolk-shell construction and corresponding schematic is provided in the inset of Fig. 6.

XRD patterns were investigated to analysis crystalline characteristics of as-prepared pure ZSH and ZSH@CQDs composites. In Fig. 7 , all diffraction peaks of ZSH match well with cubic ZSH crystal (JCPDS 73-2384). No traces of other impurity signals could be detected, suggesting high phase purity of products. The main peaks at 2θ of 19.69°, 22.78°, 32.44°, 40.01°, 46.53°, 52.42° and 57.87° can be readily indexed to the (1 1 1), (2 0 0), (2 2 0), (2 2 2), (4 0 0), (4 2 0) and (4 2 2) planes of cubic ZSH, respectively. Moreover, it is clear that diffraction peaks of ZSH are very sharp and strong, implying that the as-prepared samples have high crystallinity. After decoration of CQDs, diffraction peaks of composites are same as pure ZSH and no CQDs signals could be seen. The results confirm that the crystallinity of carbon material is inferior and introduction of it has no effect on the structure ZSH.

Photocatalytic activities of as-prepared samples were evaluated by organic dye degradation under visible light illumination. As shown in Fig. 8 a, pure ZSH catalysts present very low RhB photodegradation performance within 8 h. However, after decoration of CQDs, photocatalytic activities of ZSH@CQDs composites obviously enhance. As for CQDs modified ZSH samples, ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure exhibits the highest RhB degradation ability and 70.4% of RhB could be decomposed under visible light irradiation within 8 h. And 48.3% and 57.4% of RhB could be degraded by solid and hollow ZSH, respectively. Fig. 8b presents apparent rate constants of RhB degradation toward different catalysts. The rate constants are 0.0806, 0.1032 and 0.1430 h−1 for ZSH@CQDs with solid, hollow and yolk-shell structures, respectively, which are about 10 times higher than that of their counterparts.

Photocatalytic activities of the obtained samples were also examined by inactivation of staphylococcus aureus and the result is similar to photocatalytic degradation of RhB. In Fig. 8c, all catalysts exhibit negligible bacterial disinfection efficiencies in dark environment, indicating samples themselves have no inactivation performance to staphylococcus aureus. Fig. 8d provides the result of photocatalytic disinfection of staphylococcus aureus curves in presence of catalysts and light illumination. As can be seen from Fig. 8d, three pure ZSH samples show negligible disinfection efficiency under visible light irradiation within 16 h. After modification by CQDs, a large enhancement could be clearly observed. In composites, ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure presents the highest photocatalytic activity in comparison with other two samples and 100% inactivation of bacteria is achieved within 16 h. Bacterial colonies of re-cultured staphylococcus aureus after dealing with ZSH@CQDs yolk-shell at different time are presented in Fig. 8e.

CQDs are considered as a desirable up-conversion luminescence material. Fig. 9 presents the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of as-prepared samples excited by different wavelengths. When catalysts are excited by long-wavelength light (from 550 nm to 800 nm), up-conversion emission bands at both UV (370–420 nm) and visible regions (420–650 nm) could be clearly seen. This result suggests that the energy from up-conversion effect of CQDs could effectively transfer to ZSH, further driven its photocatalytic activity.

In addition, CQDs are also demonstrated to be an excellent electron reservoir to capture photogenerated electrons and inhibit recombination of charge carriers [31,32]. Fig. 10 a provides photocurrent-time measuring curves of as-prepared samples under several on/off visible light illumination cycles. Typically, high current intensity manifests efficient separation and migration of photoinduced charge carries. In Fig. 10a, three pure ZSH samples have no current signals, suggesting ZSH could not be excited by visible light. When CQDs are supported onto ZSH, composite systems present a large enhancement in photoinduced current signals and ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell shows the highest current intensity in comparison with that of hollow and solid.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is also a useful method to examine the differences in electron migration and recombination behaviors of materials. Fig. 10b provides typical Nyquist plots of as-prepared samples. These plots are best fitted to the equivalent Randle circuit of Rs(QRct) in inset of Fig. 10b, where Rs, Q and Rct represent electrolyte resistance, constant phase element and charge transfer resistance, respectively. Semicircle in EIS spectra is attributed to contribution from Rct and constant phase element at photocatalyst. In Fig. 10b, diameter of arc on Nyquist plot of ZSH@CQDs yolk-shell is significantly smaller than that of other materials. A low arc suggests composite materials may slow down recombination of photoinduced charge carries owing to fast interfacial charge migration in the case of materials.

In order to confirm active adducts during photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation, active radicals trapping experiment over ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure was carried out. In Fig. 11 , several scavengers including isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) and p-benzoquinone (BQ) are introduced into photocatalytic reaction system to eliminate
OH, h+ and
O2 −, respectively. In Fig. 11a, RhB elimination efficiency could be largely suppressed when EDTA-2Na and BQ are added, confirming h+ and
O2 − are two main active adducts in this process. As for photocatalytic disinfection of staphylococcus aureus (Fig. 11b), the results suggest that
OH, h+ and
O2 − simultaneously play major roles in inactivation system.

Based on the above-mentioned results, a possible photocatalytic mechanism toward ZSH@CQDs yolk-shell was reasonably proposed. As shown in Fig. 12 , CQDs act as an efficient energy converter to drive photocatalytic disinfection of bacteria and RhB degradation. Under visible light illumination, CQDs first absorb visible light, followed by shorter wavelength light emission in the range of UV light, which is further utilized by ZSH and generate photoinduced electrons and holes. Besides, CQDs could also serve as an electron migration pathway for ZSH and photogenerated electrons could easily transfer to CQDs from CB of ZSH, inhibiting electron-hole recombination and thereby improving their lifetimes [33]. Furthermore, photogenerated electrons in CQDs would be captured by O2 on surface of samples to generate active
O2 −, which is confirmed one of the main active adducts in photocatalytic disinfection of staphylococcus aureus process. Meanwhile, holes in VB of ZSH would transfer to surface of ZSH and then react with OH− to produce
OH, together with h+ are regarded as another two major active radicals, which would attack bacteria of staphylococcus aureus. As for RhB photodegradation, strong oxidative
O2 − and h+ are confirmed two main active adducts, degrading RhB to intermediates or CO2 and H2O.

In summary, carbon quantum dots decorated ZSH micro-spheres with uniform solid, hollow and yolk-shell structures were successfully synthesized by an etching-second growth strategy and facile hydrothermal method. Compared with pure ZSH, ZSH@CQDs composites exhibited obviously enhanced photocatalytic activities for both degradation of organic pollutant and disinfection of staphylococcus aureus. In CQDs decorated composites, ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure presented 70.4% of RhB degradation efficiency under visible light irradiation within 8 h, which was about 10 times higher than that of ZSH samples. ZSH@CQDs with yolk-shell structure also manifested the best photocatalytic inactivation performance under visible light illumination, which could achieve water purification of 100% bacteria disinfection within 16 h. This enhanced photocatalytic activity might ascribe to the unique up-converted PL property of CQDs, as well as its efficient charge separation. This paper could provide a new perspective to design efficient CQDs decorated photocatalysts for water purification.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Doctoral Foundation of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017BEM013) and Primary Research & Development Plan of Shandong Province (2017GNC13110).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2018.10.087.

ce:section.ce:para:
